title: Communication complexity of approximate Nash equilibria
speaker: Aviad Rubinstein
abstract: For a constant $\epsilon$, we prove a $\mathrm{poly}(N)$ lower bound on the communication complexity of $\epsilon$-Nash equilibrium in two-player $N \times N$ games. For $n$-player binary-action games we prove an $\exp(n)$ lower bound for the communication complexity of $(\epsilon,\epsilon)$-weak approximate Nash equilibrium, which is a profile of mixed actions such that at least $(1-\epsilon)$-fraction of the players are $\epsilon$-best replying.

proof_stages.label: Communication-complexity formulation of approximate equilibrium
proof_stages.summary: The talk motivates Nash equilibrium computation, replaces conditional computational hardness by unconditional communication lower bounds, states the two-player and many-player lower-bound theorems, and defines epsilon Nash and epsilon-delta weak Nash equilibria.

proof_stages.label: Totality and small-support obstacles
proof_stages.summary: Because equilibria always exist and approximate equilibria have small supports, standard communication reductions from decision problems or nondeterministic-hard problems cannot be used directly.

proof_stages.label: End-of-line hardness lifted to communication
proof_stages.summary: The proof takes end-of-line as the total-search source problem, uses a simulation/lifting theorem with an indexing gadget, and invokes the WAP model to obtain randomized communication lower bounds from query lower bounds.

proof_stages.label: Brouwer fixed-point embedding of end-of-line
proof_stages.summary: A Hirsch-Papadimitriou-Vavasis-style construction embeds the end-of-line path into a Lipschitz Brouwer map whose only approximate fixed points reveal endpoints, strengthened so being far from fixed is witnessed in many coordinates and with a default safe displacement away from decodable path regions.

proof_stages.label: Need for non-black-box composition
proof_stages.summary: Since the Brouwer reduction is continuous while the lifting theorem is discrete, the argument cannot simply compose them black-box and instead must combine both mechanisms inside one game gadget.

proof_stages.label: Imitation game turning fixed points into equilibria
proof_stages.summary: A two-player continuous imitation game is defined in which Alice tries to match Bob's vector and Bob tries to match f(x), so equilibria correspond to fixed points and the squared-norm payoff makes mixed strategies analyzable through expectations and variance.

proof_stages.label: Simulation game enforcing local distributed evaluation
proof_stages.summary: The game is augmented with decoded vertices and lifting-gadget messages alpha and beta, with payoffs forcing Bob and Alice to reveal the local information needed to compute the Brouwer displacement without giving either player the whole function.

proof_stages.label: Extensions and consequences
proof_stages.summary: The same construction is briefly adapted to many two-action players by splitting coordinates among players, and the communication lower bound is related to slow convergence of uncoupled learning dynamics.

examples.name: Combinatorial auctions with exponentially many actions
examples.note: Auctions where bidders value subsets of items illustrate why the normal-form action set size can be exponential in a smaller underlying description, motivating communication lower bounds in large games.
